skill.name: powershell-expert
skill.description: Develop PowerShell scripts, modules, GUI tools, and PowerShell Gallery workflows using documented patterns and Microsoft ecosystem guidance.
skill.summary: Build production-ready PowerShell automation, modules, GUI tools, and Gallery workflows.
skill.author: hmohamed01

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown PowerShell examples and keyword matches. The real risk is moderate: the skill includes a runnable Gallery search script and documentation that encourages module installation and repository trust decisions. No malicious intent, credential exfiltration, hidden persistence, or prompt injection text was found.

medium_findings.title: Runnable Gallery Search Script Performs Network Queries
medium_findings.description: The bundled Search-Gallery.ps1 script accepts user search parameters and calls Find-PSResource or Find-Module against PSGallery. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it still performs network lookups and depends on local PowerShell module behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly invokes Gallery search cmdlets using user-provided search values. The destination is the standard PSGallery repository, so the risk is moderate rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Module Install Guidance Can Reduce User Prompts
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes Install-PSResource examples with TrustRepository and repository trust configuration. This is common PowerShell Gallery guidance, but users could install unreviewed third-party modules if they follow it without validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The install and trust commands are explicitly documented. They match the skill domain, but they can materially change local systems by installing external packages.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Alerts Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts occur inside Markdown PowerShell examples, such as function templates, GUI snippets, and cmdlet references. These are instructional examples and are not executed by the skill unless a user chooses to run generated or copied commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are fenced Markdown examples or tables, not executable startup code. They are expected content for a PowerShell guidance skill.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Alerts Are Example-Based
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged file access patterns in examples such as Get-Content, Copy-Item, Get-Help, and GUI file browser dialogs. These examples do not read local files during skill loading and are presented as user-controlled script patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations appear in documentation or user-run examples. I found no automatic local file collection or hidden file access behavior.

low_findings.title: Publishing Example Uses Placeholder API Key
low_findings.description: The publishing section shows a placeholder API key variable and Publish-PSResource commands. This is not credential theft, but users should avoid inserting real keys into shared prompts or logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key is clearly a placeholder and no network exfiltration endpoint is present. The residual concern is user handling of real secrets when adapting the example.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to headings, tables, and ordinary PowerShell documentation text rather than hashing, encryption, or insecure cryptographic API usage. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar cryptographic operations in the inspected content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain cryptographic implementation. They are documentation headings or benign PowerShell syntax examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: PowerShell Gallery Network Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Search-Gallery.ps1 can query PSGallery through Find-PSResource or Find-Module. This is expected behavior for module discovery, but it should be disclosed because it contacts an external package repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network-capable cmdlets are directly present in the helper script. The repository target is explicit and aligned with the skill purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository Trust and Package Installation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The references include commands that trust PSGallery and install resources. These are legitimate examples, but they can affect the local environment when copied and executed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and can install external packages. The context is educational rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: PowerShell work often needs reliable structure, parameter design, and accurate module guidance. This skill provides practical templates and references for scripts, GUIs, and Gallery workflows.
actual_capabilities: Provides templates for advanced functions, parameters, pipeline input, and error handling.
Guides Windows Forms and WPF GUI creation with common control patterns.
Explains PowerShell Gallery search, install, update, save, and publish workflows.
Includes a Search-Gallery.ps1 helper for finding Gallery resources through PSResourceGet or PowerShellGet.
Recommends verification against PowerShell Gallery and Microsoft documentation when exact module details matter.
Summarizes module categories such as Azure, testing, console tools, secrets, web, and GUI modules.
limitations: GUI examples target Windows desktop environments and may not work on non-Windows systems.
Live module versions and cmdlet syntax require web or local verification.
The included Gallery search script depends on PowerShellGet or PSResourceGet availability.
Install and publish examples require user review before running against real repositories.

use_cases.title: Create Automation Scripts
use_cases.description: Draft PowerShell scripts with approved verbs, typed parameters, pipeline support, and clear error handling.

use_cases.title: Build Internal GUI Tools
use_cases.description: Create Windows Forms or WPF utilities with common controls, layout patterns, and event handlers.

use_cases.title: Manage Gallery Modules
use_cases.description: Find, install, update, save, or publish PowerShell modules using PSResourceGet and Gallery references.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PowerShell script for [task]. Include comment-based help, typed parameters, error handling, and pipeline support where useful.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Function
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this PowerShell function for naming, parameter design, ShouldProcess usage, output behavior, and error handling: [paste function].

prompt_templates.title: Design a GUI Tool
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a PowerShell GUI for [workflow]. Use Windows Forms or WPF, describe the controls, events, validation, and result handling.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Module Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a PowerShell module release for [module purpose]. Include manifest fields, exported functions, tests, Gallery metadata, and safe publishing steps.

output_examples.input: Create a script that processes server names from the pipeline.
output_examples.output: A structured script outline with comment-based help, mandatory typed parameters, begin/process/end blocks, and specific error handling guidance.

output_examples.input: Help me build a small tool that asks for a file and returns the selected path.
output_examples.output: A Windows Forms design with a file picker dialog, validation behavior, cancel handling, and a concise explanation of platform requirements.

output_examples.input: Find a module for working with Excel files in PowerShell.
output_examples.output: A recommendation workflow that verifies the Gallery package, checks current metadata, and explains install commands with user confirmation.

best_practices: Verify current module availability and cmdlet syntax before recommending exact commands.
Use typed parameters, validation attributes, and approved Verb-Noun naming for reusable functions.
Add WhatIf, Confirm, and ShouldProcess support before generating destructive operations.
anti_patterns: Do not install Gallery modules without checking source, maintenance status, and repository trust.
Do not rely on aliases or positional parameters in production scripts.
Do not claim exact module versions or command syntax without live verification.

faq.question: Does this skill run PowerShell automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and includes a helper script, but execution requires user action.

faq.question: Can it help with both scripts and modules?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers script structure, function design, module manifests, Gallery workflows, and publishing basics.

faq.question: Does it support PowerShell GUI development?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Windows Forms and WPF patterns for controls, events, layouts, and dialogs.

faq.question: Does it work outside Windows?
faq.answer: Core PowerShell guidance can apply cross-platform, but GUI examples are Windows-focused.

faq.question: How does it verify module recommendations?
faq.answer: It instructs the assistant to check PowerShell Gallery and Microsoft documentation when accuracy is critical.

faq.question: Is it safe to use install examples directly?
faq.answer: Review modules, repository trust, scope, and permissions before running install or publish commands.
